Figure 1: The SOE premium. This figure plots the difference between listed non-SOEs and listed SOEs in credit spread (left axis), estimated using quarterly regressions, controlling for credit ratings and other bond and firm characteristics. The shaded area indicates 95% confidence intervals. Also reported are the total quarterly default amounts in the credit market (right axis). [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
Figure 2: Model calibrations on the SOE premium. This figure plots the calibration results. Panel A shows the credit spreads with varying bailout probabilityπg$\pi _g$and liquidity shock intensityξ$\xi$. Panel B shows the SOE premium with varying bailout probabilityπg$\pi _g$and liquidity shock intensityξ$\xi$. The SOE premium is defined as the difference between non-SOEs (πg=0$\pi _g=0$) and SOEs with positiveπg$\pi _g$. For other parameters, see SectionII.B. (Color figure can be viewed at wileyonlinelibrary.com) [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
Figure 3: Government holdings and default measures. This figure plots government holdings and default measures. Panel A plots histograms of government holdings for four types of firms—central and local SOEs, privately owned enterprises (POEs), and other non-SOEs. Panel B plots the time series of government holdings by type, with dotted lines indicating median values and shaded areas covering the 25thto 75thpercentiles. Panels C and D plot the time series of default measures of non-SOEs (blue line) and SOEs (red line), with Panel C using Merton's model and Panel D using our unified model. The green line further plots the difference in default measure between non-SOEs and SOEs. The shaded areas in Panels C and D indicate 95% confidence intervals. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
Figure 4: Explaining the SOE premium. This figure plots the difference between listed non-SOEs and listed SOEs in credit spread, estimated using quarterly regressions, controlling for Merton's DM (blue line) and our unified DM (red line), as well as credit ratings and other bond and firm characteristics. The shaded areas indicate 95% confidence intervals. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
Figure 5: Slope coefficient from the regression of credit spreads on default measures. This figure plots the slope coefficient from the regression of credit spreads on Merton's DM and our unified DM, respectively, controlling for credit ratings and other bond and firm characteristics. The shaded areas indicate 95% confidence intervals. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
Figure 6: Quarterly ROA for non-SOEs and SOEs. Panel A plots the average quarterly ROA for non-SOEs and SOEs. ROA is net profit divided by lagged book assets. Also reported are the total quarterly default amounts in the credit market (right axis). Panel B plots the difference in quarterly ROA between non-SOEs and SOEs, using quarterly regressions, controlling for equity size and quarter and industry fixed effects. To smooth out the seasonality, we further plot the performance gap using a rolling window of eight quarters. The shaded areas indicate 95% confidence intervals. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
